segments.to: Fort Laramie, Wyoming (via the Platte River Road)
segments.cost: Wagon outfit: $600-$1,000 for wagon, ox team, provisions for 4-6 months, firearms, tools, and spare parts. Many families sold their farms to finance the trip.
segments.conditions: The trail followed the south bank of the Platte River across Nebraska Territory — flat, treeless, and baking in summer heat. Cholera was the great killer on this stretch, striking camps along the Platte in epidemic years (1849-1852) and killing victims within hours of the first cramps. Graves lined the trail by the hundreds. The river itself was undrinkable — alkali-laced and full of sand. Buffalo chips served as the only fuel for cooking fires on the treeless plains. Dust from the wagon trains ahead hung in the air like fog and coated everything — food, clothing, lungs.

segments.to: South Pass, Wyoming (Continental Divide crossing at 7,550 feet)
segments.cost: included in outfit; resupply at Fort Laramie at inflated frontier prices — flour at $1/pound, coffee at $2/pound
segments.conditions: The trail climbed from the high plains into the foothills of the Rockies, crossing the Laramie Mountains and following the Sweetwater River past Independence Rock — where emigrants carved their names into granite — and through Devil's Gate, a narrow chasm in the rock where the Sweetwater squeezed through a 370-foot notch. South Pass itself was a broad, gentle saddle that emigrants often crossed without realizing they had left the Atlantic watershed for the Pacific. Snow could fall at this altitude any month of the year.

segments.to: Sacramento or San Francisco, California (via the Humboldt River and Sierra Nevada)
segments.travel_time: 8-12 weeks, including the desert crossings and the Sierra Nevada
segments.cost: included in outfit; abandoned property along the trail (furniture, stoves, anvils) marked the desperation of teams failing on the final stretch
segments.conditions: West of South Pass, the trail descended to the Humboldt River in present-day Nevada — a sluggish, alkaline stream that emigrants followed for 300 miles through sagebrush desert before it vanished into the Humboldt Sink. The Forty-Mile Desert beyond the Sink was the worst stretch on the entire trail: no water, no shade, dead oxen and abandoned wagons lining the road, sand that swallowed wheels to the axle. Those who survived the desert faced the Sierra Nevada — granite walls rising 7,000 feet above the Sacramento Valley. The Donner Party's fate in 1846-47 haunted every emigrant who approached those passes in late autumn.

who_used_it: An estimated 250,000 people traveled the California Trail between 1841 and 1869. The first trickle of settlers in the early 1840s became a flood after gold was discovered at Sutter's Mill in January 1848 — the Forty-Niners of 1849 alone numbered over 25,000 on the overland route. The trail was used by families seeking farmland, single men seeking gold, Mormons bound for Utah (diverging at South Pass), and military expeditions. The completion of the transcontinental railroad in May 1869 rendered the wagon trail obsolete almost overnight.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} left {origin} with a wagon, an ox team, and four months of provisions, joining the great overland migration to {destination} — 2,000 miles of dust, cholera, desert, and granite mountains that killed one in ten who started.
source_references: John D. Unruh Jr., 'The Plains Across: The Overland Emigrants and the Trans-Mississippi West, 1840-60' (University of Illinois Press, 1979)
George R. Stewart, 'The California Trail: An Epic with Many Heroes' (McGraw-Hill, 1962)
Merrill J. Mattes, 'The Great Platte River Road: The Covered Wagon Mainline via Fort Kearny to Fort Laramie' (Nebraska State Historical Society, 1969)
Will Bagley, 'So Rugged and Mountainous: Blazing the Trails to Oregon and California, 1812-1848' (University of Oklahoma Press, 2010)
